name: Precedence Climbing
summary: Single-recursive-function expression parser parameterised by a minimum precedence. Provably equivalent to Pratt parsing; preferred when the operator set is small and fixed.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Precedence climbing (attributed to Keith Clarke, Martin Richards, and later Theodore Norvell) is a closely related alternative to Pratt parsing for expression grammars. Instead of keeping a table of `nud` and `led` functions keyed by token, the parser is a single recursive function parameterised by a minimum precedence:

```
fn parse_expr(min_prec):
    left = parse_atom()
    while is_binop(peek()) and prec(peek()) >= min_prec:
        op = consume()
        next_min = prec(op) + (1 if left_assoc(op) else 0)
        right = parse_expr(next_min)
        left = mk_binop(op, left, right)
    return left
```

Clang's `Parser::ParseExpression` (in `lib/Parse/ParseExpr.cpp`) is a production implementation: it calls `ParseCastExpression` for atoms and climbs precedence levels in exactly this shape.

sources.label: Eli Bendersky — Parsing Expressions by Precedence Climbing
Theodore Norvell — Parsing Expressions by Recursive Descent

sections.title: Operator handling
sections.content: The recursion threshold encodes associativity: for a left-associative operator, the next recursive call uses `prec(op) + 1`, so an operator at the same precedence terminates the inner expression; for a right-associative operator, the next recursive call uses `prec(op)`, so equal-precedence operators continue. Adjusting precedence and associativity is a table edit, not a structural change to the parser.

sections.title: Equivalence to Pratt parsing
sections.content: The equivalence with Pratt parsing is not controversial: Norvell's 2016 update explicitly states that precedence climbing and Top-Down Operator Precedence "are pretty much the same algorithm, formulated a bit differently." The practical difference is pedagogical and ergonomic — Pratt centres per-operator functions (extensibility feels like adding methods), precedence climbing centres a single control-flow function (extensibility is a table edit).

sections.title: When to prefer it
sections.content: For a language with a small, fixed operator set — C, C++, Rust expression position — precedence climbing is less code than Pratt. The whole expression parser is one function plus a precedence table. For languages that add operators late (extensible syntax systems, Haskell user-defined operators), Pratt's per-token dispatch is more natural because each new operator can carry its own behaviour without editing a central control-flow function.

sources.label: Eli Bendersky — Parsing Expressions by Precedence Climbing
Theodore Norvell — Parsing Expressions by Recursive Descent
